one_line_takeaway: In a unified EMFI hardware attack, floating-point (FP32/FP16) neural network weights collapse to near-random accuracy from a single EM pulse due to exponent-bit-driven NaN/overflow cascades, while bounded integer quantization (especially INT8) inherently 'numerically masks' the same fault density and preserves substantial accuracy, even though the underlying bit-error rate is statistically similar across formats.
key_finding: Bit error rate is largely format-independent (mean BER 5.97-7.51% across FP32/FP16/INT8/INT4, Table IV), meaning the number of induced faults is roughly constant regardless of representation -- so the large accuracy gap between formats is not explained by more faults but by how each representation *responds* to a given fault. FP32 and FP16 models collapse to near-random-guess accuracy after a single EM pulse in most tested cases, driven by exponent-bit corruption: 1.27+/-0.32% of FP32 weights per chunk become NaN post-attack (vs ~0.10% for FP16, ~10x lower due to fewer exponent bits), and the weight range expands by a factor of ~10^39 for FP32 and ~10^6 for FP16 (no +/-infinity values were observed -- the 0xFF exponent pattern produces quiet NaNs). INT8 and INT4 remain bounded by the quantization scale and are structurally immune to this NaN/overflow cascade, even though corrupted bytes in integer formats hit the maximal 0xFE/0xFF value more often than in floating point (7.1% INT8, 9.2% INT4 of corrupted bytes vs 2.9% FP32, 1.0% FP16 -- Fig. 8) -- clamping bounds the damage instead of exploding it. The paper's headline number: on the largest network tested, VGG-11 in INT8 retains ~70% Top-1 and ~90% Top-5 accuracy after a single EMFI injection (vanilla VGG-11 INT8 baseline is 68.19% Top-1 / 88.65% Top-5 per Table III, so accuracy is essentially preserved in many chunk locations). Spatial analysis (Table VI) shows a front-heavy vulnerability for ResNets (attacking front chunks drives Top-1 to ~0.05-0.15% regardless of format) and a bimodal front/back vulnerability for VGG-11 (front and back chunks catastrophic, e.g. VGG11-INT8 front 0.15% vs middle 68.14%), explained by the large, redundant FC6 layer (4096x25088, ~98MB in INT8) diluting the effect of any single 4MB fault window to ~0.6% of its weights.
core_contribution: This is the paper's own primary contribution (not related work): a controlled, head-to-head electromagnetic fault injection (EMFI) study comparing four weight number formats -- FP32, FP16, INT8, INT4 -- across four CNN architectures (ResNet-18, ResNet-34, ResNet-50, VGG-11) trained on ImageNet-1K, under a single unified hardware/attack setup (NewAE ChipSHOUTER, 300V/160ns pulse, single pulse per attack, 4MB SRAM DUT). Rather than only measuring post-attack accuracy, the authors characterize the fault mechanism itself: bit error rate (BER) per format, spatial/logical distribution of corrupted bytes, prevalence of 0xFE/0xFF byte values, and floating-point-specific NaN/range-explosion statistics. They also perform a spatial (front/middle/back layer) sensitivity analysis by replacing individual 4MB chunks (352 unique fault-injected models total). The authors state this is 'the first comparative study of neural network fault-injection robustness across multiple weight precisions under a unified experimental setup.'
threat_model: Physical, non-invasive hardware attacker with proximity access to the weight-storage memory of an embedded/edge device (an 'in-storage fault-injection threat model'): the adversary uses a high-voltage EM pulse generator (ChipSHOUTER, 200-500V pulses) and an XYZ-positioned injection probe to induce transient bit flips in an external SRAM chip holding model weights, either before or during device boot. Inference itself is run separately on an unmodified GPU (ONNX Runtime/CUDA) to isolate representation-level effects from confounds like memory interleaving, caching, and ECC found in integrated SoCs -- the paper is explicit this is a deliberate simplification, not full in-situ microcontroller inference. This sits within the established neural-network fault-injection attack taxonomy the paper reviews: misclassification (untargeted/targeted), model extraction via bit flips, and backdoor/trojan planting -- but the paper's own experiments target only accuracy-degradation/misclassification outcomes, not extraction or backdooring.
limitations: Explicitly acknowledged by the authors: (1) inference is performed on a separate, unmodified GPU rather than in-situ on the target microcontroller, isolating representation-level effects but not capturing confounds (memory interleaving, instruction/cache effects, ECC) present in real integrated SoCs -- flagged as future work; (2) the 4MB memory capacity of the test platform is modest compared to production AI accelerators, a deliberate choice to avoid confounds from memory interleaving/redundancy/ECC in more integrated systems, but it limits generalization to those systems; (3) only a single EM pulse per attack and only one specific pulse configuration (300V/160ns) were used, so the results characterize one operating point of the attack rather than a sweep; (4) results for architectures/formats not shown in Table VI (several ResNet configurations) were omitted because post-attack accuracy was uniformly too low for meaningful spatial conclusions, limiting the granularity of the spatial analysis for those cases; (5) only classification/misclassification (accuracy degradation) is evaluated experimentally -- targeted misclassification, model extraction, and backdoor implantation (all discussed in the paper's background/taxonomy) are not tested by this paper's own experiments.

datasets_used: ImageNet-1K (ILSVRC) validation set, sub-sampled to 4,096 images with fixed random seed 21 for reproducibility; 1,000 classes so random-guess baseline is 0.1%
quantitative_results: Mean bit error rate (BER) per format across all fault-injected chunks (Table IV): FP32 5.97% (std 0.36), FP16 6.07% (std 0.72), INT8 7.51% (std 0.51), INT4 6.15% (std 1.30) -- format-independent overall (narrow 5.97-7.51% range)
0xFE/0xFF corrupted-byte fraction (Fig. 8): FP32 ~2.9%, FP16 ~1.0%, INT8 ~7.1%, INT4 ~9.2% of corrupted bytes per chunk

Floating-point corruption stats (Table V): FP32 NaN fraction 1.27+/-0.32% per chunk (range 1.17-1.30%), 0% Inf, range expansion factor ~10^39; FP16 NaN fraction ~0.10+/-0.01%, 0% Inf, range expansion factor ~10^6
Surface scan: most sensitive probe location reached ~15% success rate (proportion of corrupted SRAM bytes) at coordinate (7,35) over an 800-point (20x40) grid
352 unique fault-injected models evaluated in total across all architecture x format x chunk combinations

Spatial sensitivity, Top-1% by chunk region (Table VI): ResNet18-INT8 (3 chunks) Front 0.07%/Middle 0.68%/Back 6.37%; ResNet34-INT8 (6 chunks) Front 0.10%/Middle 0.12%/Back 53.05%; ResNet50-INT8 (7 chunks) Front 0.15%/Middle 5.20%/Back 49.10%; ResNet50-INT4 (4 chunks) Front 0.12%/Middle 0.17%/Back 22.19%; VGG11-FP32 (127 chunks) Front 0.05%/Middle 0.05%/Back 0.12%; VGG11-FP16 (64 chunks) Front 0.05%/Middle 0.05%/Back 62.84%; VGG11-INT8 (32 chunks) Front 0.15%/Middle 68.14%/Back 54.13%; VGG11-INT4 (16 chunks) Front 0.07%/Middle 65.03%/Back 20.21%

Headline resilience result: on VGG-11 (largest network tested), INT8 quantization retained Top-1 accuracy of around 70% and Top-5 around 90% under EMFI attack conditions where it was measured, versus near-collapse for FP32/FP16

VGG-11 FC6 layer geometry used to explain bimodal INT8/INT4 sensitivity: shape 4096x25088, ~98MB in INT8 and ~49MB in INT4; a 4MB fault window covers ~4% of FC6 bytes, and at ~15% observed byte-corruption rate this yields ~0.6% of FC6 weights modified per injection
baselines_compared: The paper's own four weight-representation configurations are compared against each other (FP32 and FP16 as baselines/higher-precision references vs INT8 and INT4 as quantized formats) -- there is no external/prior defense or attack baseline reproduced experimentally in this paper; comparisons to prior EMFI/fault-injection studies (Hong et al. bit-flip attack reducing accuracy >90% on FP32; Rakin et al. bit-flip attack degrading INT8/6/4 models to ~0% accuracy; Libano et al. radiation-induced faults on FPGA binary vs FP32 CNNs showing 39% fewer errors but 12% more catastrophic ones for binary; Guillemé et al. SFI4NN framework and a redundancy countermeasure improving fault tolerance by ~96%; Gaine et al. EMFI/laser instruction-skip attack on ARM Cortex-M4; Goswami et al. EMFI on TinyML/FRAM causing up to 40% accuracy loss) are discussed narratively in the paper's own Table I as prior/related work, not reproduced as experimental baselines in this paper's methodology.
